ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Bamboo Pith and Silver Fungus Soup. Mapped to broader class 'soup (food product)' as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor. Bamboo pith (Dictyophora indusiata) and silver fungus (Tremella fuciformis) are specialty fungal ingredients without dedicated FoodOn URIs.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Bamboo Pith and Silver Fungus Soup. Code A00GJ (Mushrooms and other fungi) is used as the closest defensible anchor given the primary fungal ingredients.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Chinese fungal soup. SNOMED CT does not maintain food composition codes at this specificity.

main_ingredients: vegetable stock or dried scallop broth

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is one bowl of approximately 250 g, typical for a side soup course at a Chinese banquet or family meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mushrooms, raw (closest approved composite analogue for fungal soup); supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables for Southeast Asian fungal ingredients
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Bamboo Pith and Silver Fungus Soup exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for raw mushrooms (FDC ID 169251) and Tremella fuciformis compositional data from FAO/INFOODS SEA tables, adjusted for the dilute broth matrix (~90% water content of the finished soup). Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against these sources and retained where consistent. Vitamin A (35 µg RAE) is noted as higher than typical for a fungal soup and is flagged as uncertain; it may reflect beta-carotene contribution from broth vegetables. Sodium 120 mg/100 g is plausible for a lightly salted broth. All values represent a reasonable estimate for a clear fungal soup at approximately 25 kcal/100 g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of fungal polysaccharide-rich foods — analogous estimate based on Tremella fuciformis and dietary fibre composition; reference: University of Sydney GI Database entries for low-GI mushroom and vegetable soups
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Bamboo Pith and Silver Fungus Soup exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 20 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) the University of Sydney GI Database entries for mushroom-based soups and high-fibre vegetable broths (GI range 15–25); (2) the high soluble polysaccharide content of Tremella fuciformis, which is documented in peer-reviewed literature to attenuate postprandial glucose response; (3) the very low available carbohydrate content (~4.8 g/100 g, predominantly fibre). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (20 × 12.0) / 100 = 2.4 (available carbohydrate per 250 g serving ≈ 12 g). This is classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium broth with meaningful potassium content
hypertension.guidance: At 120 mg sodium per 100 g (300 mg per 250 g serving), this soup is relatively low in sodium for a broth-based dish; individuals with hypertension should nonetheless monitor total daily sodium intake and avoid adding soy sauce or salty condiments, while the 110 mg/100 g potassium content provides a modest vasodilatory benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and high soluble dietary fibre from Tremella polysaccharides
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 20 and a glycemic load of approximately 2.4 per 250 g serving, this soup has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose; the soluble beta-glucan polysaccharides in Tremella fuciformis have been shown in peer-reviewed studies to slow glucose absorption, making this a suitable soup choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided rock sugar or sweeteners are not added.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and bioactive fungal polysaccharides
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This soup contains negligible saturated fat (~0.03 g/100 g) and zero cholesterol, and the bioactive polysaccharides from both Dictyophora indusiata and Tremella fuciformis have been associated in preliminary research with lipid-modulating and anti-inflammatory effects; it is a heart-friendly soup choice when prepared with low-sodium stock and without added oils.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polysaccharides in both fungal ingredients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Both bamboo pith and silver fungus are rich in bioactive polysaccharides with potential immune-modulating and anti-inflammatory properties; no specific cautions apply for rheumatoid arthritis patients consuming this soup in standard serving sizes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bamboo Pith and Silver Fungus Soup (竹笙银耳汤, Zhú Shēng Yín Ěr Tāng) is a refined and highly prized clear soup in Chinese cuisine, traditionally served at high-end banquets and formal occasions, particularly in southern China. The two star ingredients are bamboo pith (Dictyophora indusiata, also called Long Net Stinkhorn), a rare edible fungus distinguished by its delicate white lacy veil and porous, spongy texture that absorbs broth flavours beautifully, and silver fungus (Tremella fuciformis, also called snow mushroom), which contributes a soft, gelatinous, almost translucent texture. The soup is prepared by simmering both fungi in a clear, lightly seasoned broth — typically a high-quality vegetable stock or a broth enriched with dried scallops — until the ingredients are tender and the liquid is crystalline. The flavour profile is subtle, clean, and delicate, with a mild umami depth from the broth. Nutritionally, the soup is very low in calories (~25 kcal/100 g), low in fat (0.2 g/100 g), and provides a meaningful amount of dietary fibre (2.2 g/100 g), predominantly as soluble polysaccharides from Tremella fuciformis. These polysaccharides are associated in Traditional Chinese Medicine with nourishing Yin, moisturising the lungs, and enhancing skin complexion. Clinically, the soup has a very low estimated glycemic index (~20) and glycemic load (~2.4 per 250 g serving), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Its low saturated fat and zero cholesterol content support cardiovascular health. Sodium content is moderate at 120 mg/100 g and should be monitored in hypertensive individuals. The soup is naturally vegetarian and vegan when prepared without dried scallops, and is gluten-free and dairy-free. In TCM, both ingredients are classified as cooling foods and are recommended as a summer wellness soup, though they are traditionally avoided during episodes of cold or diarrhoea.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Bamboo Pith and Silver Fungus Soup exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central mushroom entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA fungal ingredient data, adjusted for the dilute broth matrix. The GI value is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable low-GI fungal soups. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE: No exact approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central (mushrooms, raw; FDC ID 169251) and FAO/INFOODS SEA fungal ingredient tables, adjusted for ~90% water content of the finished soup. Input AI-generated values were cross-checked and retained where consistent with these sources. 2. VITAMIN A: The input value of 35 µg RAE/100 g is higher than typical for a pure fungal soup and may reflect beta-carotene contribution from broth vegetables; this value is flagged as uncertain and should be verified against a laboratory analysis if precision is required. 3. VITAMIN B12: vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as fungi and plant-based broths contain no vitamin B12; this is naturally zero for a vegan fungal soup. 4. CHOLESTEROL: cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero for a plant-based fungal soup with no animal-derived ingredients (when prepared without dried scallops). 5. TRANS FAT: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero; no hydrogenated fats are used in this preparation. 6. MONOUNSATURATED AND POLYUNSATURATED FAT: Values of 0.02 g and 0.08 g respectively are estimated from USDA mushroom fatty acid profiles; detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific soup is not available in approved sources. 7. WATER: water_g set to 91.0 g/100 g — estimated for a clear broth-based soup; not directly reported in approved sources. 8. GI: GI of 20 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for low-GI mushroom and vegetable soups; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists. 9. ALLERGENS: Dried scallops (shellfish) are a common optional ingredient; the soup is vegan/shellfish-free when prepared with vegetable stock only. 10. PORTIONS: The input contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical 250 g entries); deduplicated to a single 250 g serving. 11. DIETARY FLAGS: is_vegetarian and is_vegan set to true for the standard vegetable-stock preparation; these flags should be set to false if dried scallops are used.
